1: YHWH sent Nathan to David. The prophet is sent; he does not go on his own authority. Christ makes the same claim about himself: 'I have not come on my own authority' (John 8:42); 'the word that you hear is not mine but the Father's' (John 14:24). Nathan embodies the prophetic pattern that Christ fulfills — the divine messenger sent to speak YHWH's word, whose rejection is the rejection of the one who sent him (Matthew 21:37).
2: The parable's rich man has very many flocks and herds. The contrast between the rich man's abundance and the poor man's single lamb anticipates Christ's own parable of the lost sheep: the shepherd who has a hundred sheep and leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one (Luke 15:4-6). Both parables present a single creature of great value to its owner; both parables are designed to generate a verdict that the teller then applies to the listener.
3: The poor man's one lamb — bought, raised alongside his children, sharing his food and cup, lying in his arms, like a daughter to him. This is the OT's most vivid image of the covenant bond between a creature and its keeper. As a type, the lamb points to Christ, the Lamb of God: innocent, beloved, taken; 'like a lamb that is led to the slaughter' (Isaiah 53:7). The poor man's attachment to the lamb expresses the Father's attachment to the Son who is taken.
4: The rich man spares his own flock to take the poor man's lamb. The verb ḥāmal (to spare) — the same verb used for Saul sparing Agag (1 Samuel 15:9) — drives the moral point. Paul uses the same logic in reverse for the atonement: 'He who did not spare (pheidomai) his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32) — God did not do what the rich man did; he gave what was most his own, not what belonged to another.
5: David burns with anger and pronounces the death verdict: 'As YHWH lives, the man who has done this deserves to die.' The one who judges others by the law condemns himself, since he has broken the same law — the theological point Paul makes in Romans 2:1: 'Therefore you have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges.' The law's function is to bring accusation; Nathan's parable is the law doing its work on David, just as the law's accusation reaches every hearer.
6: The fourfold restoration cites Exodus 22:1 — David applies the Torah with precision to the man in the parable before knowing it applies to himself. Christ's statement in Matthew 7:2 — 'with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged' — is the underlying principle: the standard David applied to the rich man is applied to him. The law's accusation works through the conscience before it names itself.
7: attah ha-ish: 'You are the man!' — the three-word collapse of the distance between judge and defendant. The confrontation pattern is Christological in two directions: Christ as the prophet who says to every listener 'you are the man/woman' — the one who does not condone the sin of the audience (cf. John 8:10-11: 'Neither do I condemn you; go and sin no more'); and Christ on the cross as the one against whom the verdict 'deserves to die' is applied, absorbing the sentence David had pronounced: 'Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree' (Galatians 3:13).
8: 'If that were too little, I would have added to you as much more.' The divine willingness to give more than was given — YHWH's grace was never exhausted — makes David's theft of Bathsheba not a sin of deprivation but of ingratitude and unchecked desire. Paul makes the same point about the surpassing generosity of Christ: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' (Romans 8:32). The logic is: given the greater gift (the Son), the lesser gifts follow freely.
9: 'You have despised the word of YHWH.' In John 1:1, Christ is the Word of YHWH. Despising YHWH's command is despising the one who embodies and speaks it — a connection Jesus makes explicit: 'The one who rejects me and does not receive my words has a judge; the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day' (John 12:48). The sin Nathan identifies as the root of David's crime is the same root of all sin: treating YHWH's word as something that can be set aside when it conflicts with desire.
10: 'The sword shall never depart from your house.' The judicial consequence of David's sword-use against Uriah is a sword that will not leave. Christ absorbs the sword of divine judgment at the cross — the curse of the law fell on him (Galatians 3:13), the sword of YHWH's justice was poured out on him (Zechariah 13:7: 'Strike the shepherd'). For those in Christ, the judicial sword of condemnation has been exhausted: 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1).
11: 'I will raise up evil against you from your own house.' The sword that comes from David's own house — Amnon, Absalom, Adonijah — is the historical fulfillment. The pattern has a typological dimension: the rejection Christ faced also came from 'his own' (John 1:11: 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him'). The household's rebellion against the chosen king recurs at every level of the Davidic narrative.
12: 'For you did it secretly, but I will do this thing before all Israel and before the sun.' The public unmasking of what was done privately is the principle of the last judgment: 'nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest, nor is anything secret that will not be known and come to light' (Luke 8:17). The cross itself is a form of public exposure — the sin David committed in secret was placed publicly on Christ, who 'made a public example' of the powers and authorities (Colossians 2:15).
13: Forensic forgiveness: 'YHWH also has put away your sin; you shall not die.' The immediate passing-over of David's guilt (heʿĕḇîr) is the OT act that generates Ps 32, which Paul quotes in Romans 4:7-8 as proof that justification is not by works: 'Blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin.' The forgiveness of David's sin in 2 Samuel 12:13 — immediate, unconditional, without a prescribed sacrifice — is one of the OT's clearest anticipations of the NT's doctrine of justification by faith apart from works of the law. It rests on the same divine mercy that the cross will make permanently available.
14: 'Nevertheless... the child who is born to you shall die.' The forgiveness is real; the consequences are not cancelled. The distinction between judicial guilt (removed, v13) and temporal consequences (continuing, v14) is part of the biblical theology of discipline: 'For the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives' (Hebrews 12:6, citing Proverbs 3:11-12). Christ suffered not disciplinary consequences (he had no sin) but penal consequences (bearing others' guilt) — the distinction matters for understanding both David's situation and the atonement.
15: YHWH struck the child and it became sick. The narrative does not present the child's death as unjust — the death is within the framework of the judicial decree of v14. The deeper Christological connection is that Christ takes not just the guilt but the consequences: 'he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows' (Isaiah 53:4). The child's death is the specific temporal consequence; Christ bears the ultimate spiritual consequence (the death that separation from God deserves).
16: David sought God on behalf of the child — fasting, lying on the ground. The king as intercessor for his people's life is the pattern that Christ fulfills completely: 'He always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). David's intense intercession was bounded by human limitation; the child died. Christ's intercession is bounded by nothing — 'he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him.'
17: The servants try to raise David but he refuses. The king will not be comforted while the child is still possibly alive — his intercession continues without concession until the outcome is known. The image of the king who will not rise from his prostration until the answer comes reflects the pattern of effective prayer that will not give up: 'Will he not give justice to his elect who cry to him day and night?' (Luke 18:7).
18: On the seventh day the child died. The servants were afraid to tell David. The silence of those who know the answer before the king is told parallels the disciples' uncertainty about the resurrection — did Christ rise? The king will have to be told, and his response will be unexpected. The seventh day (completion number) marks the terminus of the crisis.
19: David perceived from his servants' behavior that the child was dead. His discernment of hidden knowledge through reading signs around him is a characteristic wisdom trait. Christ's knowledge of what was in people's hearts — 'knowing their thoughts' (Matthew 9:4; 2 Samuel 12:25) — is the divine fulfillment of this discerning wisdom, which in David is partial and human.
20: David arose, washed, anointed, changed clothes, went to the house of YHWH, and worshiped — then he ate. The sequence has a resurrection-pattern structure: arising from the ground, washing (ritual purification), going to the place of YHWH, worship. The movement from desolate prostration to the house of YHWH parallels the pattern of Christ's resurrection and return to the Father — the one who was laid low rises to worship and communion.
21: The servants' question — 'you fasted and wept while he lived, but rose and ate when he died?' — expresses the conventional expectation: grief follows death, relief precedes it. David inverts this. The pattern is the NT's resurrection inversion: mourning is followed by resurrection joy (John 16:20: 'your sorrow will turn to joy'), and those who weep now will laugh (Luke 6:21). David's restoration of appetite and worship after the child's death anticipates the resurrection pattern of grief turned to feasting.
22: 'Who knows whether YHWH will be gracious to me?' — the prayer of mî yōdēaʿ (who knows?) is the ground of petitionary prayer before a free and gracious God. In Christ, the uncertainty is resolved: 'Let us therefore approach the throne of grace with confidence (parrēsia), so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in time of need' (Hebrews 4:16). David prays from the hope that YHWH might be gracious; Hebrews calls believers to approach with certainty, because the high priest has already interceded.
23: Resurrection hope: 'I will go to him, but he will not return to me.' David's confidence that he will go to where the dead child is — ʾănî hōlēk ʾēlāyw (I am going to him) — is the OT's most personal expression of anticipatory afterlife hope. Christ makes this explicit: 'I go to prepare a place for you... I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also' (John 14:2-3). David's one-directional hope ('I will go to him') is fulfilled in Christ's two-directional promise: he went first; he returns to bring us where he is.
24: Solomon is born to David and Bathsheba. From the grief of the lost child, the covenant continues through a new son. The pattern is one of substitution and continuation — one child lost, a new one given who will carry the promise. Christ is the ultimate covenant-continuation son: born into a situation of sin, failure, and death in the Davidic line, he is the son through whom the promise is definitively fulfilled (Matthew 1:1: 'the book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, son of David').
25: Jedidiah, beloved of YHWH: 'YHWH loved him and sent a word by Nathan the prophet. So he called his name Jedidiah, because of YHWH.' The name yəḏîḏyāh (beloved of YHWH) is YHWH's own naming of Solomon. The Father's voice at Jesus's baptism — 'This is my beloved (agapētos) Son, in whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17) — uses the Greek equivalent of yāḏîḏ. The Davidic son who is called 'beloved of YHWH' and who will build the temple is the type; the Son who is the Father's beloved and who builds the temple of his body (John 2:19-21) is the antitype.
26: Joab fought against Rabbah and took the city of waters. Joab accomplishes the decisive military action and then summoned David to take the credit. The servant completing the work that the king will then claim is a pattern that runs through the Davidic narrative. Christ, conversely, is both the one who accomplished the work and the one who receives the honor: 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — he does not hand off to another to claim the victory.
27: Joab sends word: come and take the city, lest it be called by my name. Joab's self-effacement before the king — refusing to let his own name be the memorial of the conquest — points to the posture of the servant before his lord. John the Baptist articulates the same posture: 'He must increase, but I must decrease' (John 3:30). The servant deflects the glory to the king; the king receives it and grants it to his people.
28: David gathers the remaining forces, comes to Rabbah, and takes it. The king appears at the decisive moment to complete what the servant began. The pattern parallels Christ's return at the consummation: the work of history (mission, suffering, witness) is the servant's preparation; the king's appearing finalizes the victory. 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory' (Matthew 25:31) is the completion of what his servants have labored toward.
29: David took Rabbah and its cities and the people returned. The gathering of spoil and return to Jerusalem after victory is the pattern of the victorious king entering his city in triumph. Christ's resurrection and ascension is the NT fulfillment: 'When he ascended on high, he led captives in his train and gave gifts to his people' (Ephesians 4:8, citing Psalms 68:18). The ascension is the victor's entry into the heavenly Jerusalem.
30: The Ammonite king's crown placed on David's head. The crown of the defeated enemy placed on the victor is the image that Revelation multiplies for Christ: 'on his head are many crowns' (Revelation 19:12). The Davidic king crowned in victory over the nations is the historical type of the eschatological king who receives the crowns of all who have opposed him and who rules 'King of kings and Lord of lords' (Revelation 19:16).
31: The Ammonites put to labor with saws, iron picks, and brick kilns. The conquered people subjected to labor for the king echoes Paul's eschatological picture: 'He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet' (1 Corinthians 15:25). The subjugation of enemy nations to the Davidic king's purposes is the temporal enactment of the ultimate subjugation when 'every knee shall bow' (Philippians 2:10) and all things are placed under the feet of the Son.
